_purpose: Courthouse Atlas for Dorchester County, MD. Harriet Tubman's county — where the records of slavery and the records of freedom coexist in the same courthouse. Dorchester's records survive reasonably well from 1669, with no catastrophic fire, but the records of the enslaved community are fragmentary by design — the system that enslaved people recorded them as property, not as persons. The Tubman connection and the large free Black population before the Civil War make Dorchester's records uniquely important for African American genealogy.

current_courthouse.location: Cambridge, MD — 206 High Street (expanded; the 1854 courthouse is the core structure, with additions)
records_housed: wills and estates — some early records at Maryland State Archives

previous_courthouses.location: Cambridge — successive frame and brick structures on or near the current courthouse site.
previous_courthouses.fate: Replaced by the current 1854 courthouse. Records transferred. No catastrophic loss documented during the transitions.

record_losses.cause: Incomplete record-keeping in the earliest settlement period; some deterioration from age and poor storage
records_destroyed: some land records from the earliest decades incomplete
records_survived: court proceedings from the 1680s onward substantially complete
wills and probate from the 1680s onward
record_losses.description: Dorchester's record losses are minor — the county avoided catastrophic fire and military destruction. The earliest records (1669-1680) are fragmentary, but from the 1680s onward the documentary record is continuous. The more significant 'loss' is systemic: enslaved people were documented as property in probate inventories and tax records, not as persons with family relationships.
record_losses.narrative_impact: The records survive — but what they record about the enslaved community is limited by design. {ancestor_name}'s name may appear in a probate inventory as a line item with a dollar value, and that may be the only documentation of a person's existence.
record_losses.source: Maryland State Archives. Guide to Government Records. Annapolis, 2005.

record_losses.cause: Systemic — free Black families' records were maintained under a hostile legal system that did not guarantee their preservation
records_destroyed: freedom certificates lost or stolen (individual losses, not institutional)
some manumission records not formally filed
records_survived: manumission records in county deed books
freedom certificates filed with the county court
federal census records listing 'free colored' individuals
record_losses.description: Dorchester County had one of the largest free Black populations on the Eastern Shore. Free Black individuals were required to carry freedom certificates and file them with the county court. Some certificates survive in the county records; others were lost, stolen, or never properly filed. The fragility of these records was a feature, not a bug — the system made it easy to challenge a free person's status and difficult for them to prove it.
record_losses.narrative_impact: A free Black ancestor in Dorchester County may or may not appear in the freedom certificate records. The absence of a certificate does not mean the person was not free — it may mean the certificate was lost, or never properly filed, or deliberately destroyed.
record_losses.source: Fields, Barbara J. Slavery and Freedom on the Middle Ground. Yale University Press, 1985.

deeds.completeness: Good. Land records from 1669, the county's founding. Provincial patent books at the State Archives supplement the earliest records.
deeds.gaps: Minor gaps in the 1670s-1680s. Substantially complete from the 1690s onward.
deeds.repository: Maryland State Archives; Dorchester County Circuit Court, Cambridge

wills_estates.completeness: Good. Wills and inventories from 1680 onward. Probate inventories are critical for identifying enslaved individuals — inventories list enslaved people by name, age (sometimes), and value.
wills_estates.repository: Maryland State Archives; Dorchester County Register of Wills

marriages.completeness: Marriage licenses from 1780. Earlier marriages documented in church records.
marriages.repository: Maryland State Archives; Dorchester County Clerk of Court

court_minutes.completeness: Good from 1680s onward. Court records document criminal proceedings, civil disputes, manumissions, apprenticeship indentures, and slave-related legal actions.
court_minutes.online_access: Maryland State Archives; Archives of Maryland Online

vital_records.repository: Maryland Division of Vital Records; Maryland State Archives

substitute_sources.source_type: Harriet Tubman Historical and Genealogical Sources
substitute_sources.repository: Maryland State Archives; Dorchester County Historical Society; Harriet Tubman Underground Railroad National Historical Park
substitute_sources.description: The Tubman family records — Brodess family papers, manumission records, court petitions for freedom, runaway advertisements — document the specific enslaved community from which Tubman emerged. These records are relevant for any family connected to the Brodess, Thompson, or Pattison plantations in Dorchester County.
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Critical for families connected to the Tubman network. The court records from the Brodess estate are among the most studied slavery documents in Maryland.

substitute_sources.description: Labor contracts, marriage registrations, complaints, and school records from the Freedmen's Bureau in Dorchester County. Dorchester's large formerly enslaved population generated a substantial volume of Bureau records.
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Essential for Black families. These records document family relationships that the slavery system refused to record.

substitute_sources.repository: Maryland State Archives; Dorchester County deed books
substitute_sources.description: Manumission records filed in the county deed books document individual acts of emancipation. Many Eastern Shore manumissions were conditional — freedom granted after a specified term of service or upon reaching a certain age.
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: High. Manumission records name both the enslaver and the enslaved person, sometimes with family relationships. Conditional manumissions document the transition from slavery to freedom over time.

substitute_sources.repository: Lovely Lane Museum, Baltimore; Baltimore-Washington Conference Archives
substitute_sources.description: Methodist circuit rider journals and quarterly conference records for the Dorchester circuit. Methodist congregations in Dorchester included both white and Black members before and after the Civil War.
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Good. Membership rolls and quarterly conference minutes document families — including Black families — in a region where civil vital records do not begin until 1898.

narrative_hooks: The Dorchester County courthouse holds the records of {ancestor_name}'s bondage — a probate inventory where a human being is listed between the livestock and the furniture — and the records of {pronoun_possessive} freedom, if freedom came.

Harriet Tubman's county kept records — the Brodess estate papers, the manumission books, the runaway advertisements — and those records document a world where {ancestor_name}'s family was either free, enslaved, or running.

The Freedmen's Bureau in Cambridge recorded the marriages that slavery had refused to record — and {ancestor_name}'s family connection, known for a generation in the community, was finally written down.

The freedom certificate in the county deed book is the most important document in {ancestor_name}'s life — without it, freedom was a word; with it, freedom was a paper that could be lost, stolen, or challenged.
source_references: Maryland State Archives. Guide to Government Records. Annapolis, 2005.
Fields, Barbara J. Slavery and Freedom on the Middle Ground. New Haven: Yale University Press, 1985.
Larson, Kate Clifford. Bound for the Promised Land: Harriet Tubman. New York: Ballantine Books, 2004.
National Archives. Records of the Bureau of Refugees, Freedmen, and Abandoned Lands (Record Group 105).
